project_description.text: The Kendall Square Urban Renewal Project, administered by the Cambridge Redevelopment Authority, would complete redevelopment of an approximately 22-24 acre cleared site in eastern Cambridge, Massachusetts, bounded by Main Street, Third Street, Binney Street, and the Penn Central/Boston and Albany Railroad tracks. The Preferred Plan proposes a mix of light industry, technical and general office space, retail, a hotel, market-rate housing, a new fire station for Engine 7, a parking garage, and about 2.5 acres of public open space, supported by major street improvements including elimination of the Kendall Square rotary, new Central and Western Connectors, and a relocated MBTA bus terminal. The project requests a $40 million Federal Capital Grant with a $6.4 million local share and would be marketed by 1990, generating roughly 5,830 to 7,970 permanent jobs depending on the alternative.

affected_community.text: The project affects the City of Cambridge and four surrounding neighborhoods, including East Cambridge (a traditional blue-collar manufacturing area with a Portuguese population), the MIT-dominated area, a lower-middle-income blue-collar neighborhood, and a low-income area with significant Black and Hispano populations. Together these neighborhoods house about 25,000 residents and 32,000 employees concentrated at MIT, Technology Square, Draper Laboratory, and other major employers, with lower median incomes, higher unemployment, and more blue-collar workers than the Cambridge or Boston averages. About 10 remaining families and six businesses still need to be relocated, and the surrounding area contains historically significant structures such as the Margaret Fuller House and the Boardman School.

alternatives_overview.text: Five alternatives are evaluated: the Preferred (Neighborhood) Plan endorsed by the City Council with a mix of industrial, office, retail, hotel, and 500-606 housing units; the Task Force Plan at about half the density and primarily industrial with no housing or hotel; the City Manager's (CCM) Plan, similar to the Preferred Plan but without residential uses; the Maximum Plan, with roughly 30 percent greater density that would eliminate the Central Connector and overload traffic; and the No-Build Alternative, leaving the site as landscaped open space. Because the area is about 95 percent already cleared, no alternative sites were considered.

environmental_impact.text: Build Plans would eliminate blight, create roughly 3,820-6,650 new jobs, expand the tax base (reducing the tax rate by $1.63-$6.46), improve aesthetics and utilities, and stabilize surrounding neighborhoods, while combined-sewer separation would improve Charles River and Boston Harbor water quality. Adverse impacts include relocation of six remaining businesses, possible indirect displacement of renters in East Cambridge, about a 15 percent increase in vehicle traffic crossing the project boundary with several intersections operating at or above capacity, increased hydrocarbon and nitrogen oxide emissions, and noise along Broadway, Main Street, and the Connectors that is Normally Unacceptable to Unacceptable under HUD standards. Construction over up to 10 years will produce fugitive dust, exhaust, pile-driving noise up to 80 dBA at nearby residences, and potential dewatering settlement within about a city block, while no known archaeological or historic sites lie within the previously cleared Project Area.

public_response.text: Community opposition forced abandonment of a 1972 concept plan emphasizing intensive office, commercial, hotel, and luxury housing, leading the City Council to pass an order in July 1973 prioritizing blue-collar and nonprofessional white-collar jobs for Cambridge residents and to establish a Task Force to advise on community concerns. The Neighborhood Plan, supported by the East Cambridge Planning Team, MIT, and the Kendall Square Businessmen's Association, was endorsed in principle by the City Council in October 1974 as the Preferred Plan. Public participation including a December 14, 1976 hearing and about 20 community meetings drew limited attendance, with citizens raising concerns about loss of working-class character, housing displacement, traffic and truck impacts, parking spillover, building density and height, school overcrowding, and competition with existing retail; the Cambridge Tenants Organizing Committee questioned the consultant's qualifications and warned the plan would push out low-income families.

value.name: Neighborhood Plan (Preferred Plan)
value.description: The City Council-endorsed preferred plan providing a broad mix of uses including light industry, technical and general office, retail, hotel, residential, and parking, with approximately 100,000 sq ft of public open space. Designated as the minimum acceptable development for the 24-acre Kendall Square urban renewal site.

value.name: Task Force Plan
value.description: A lower-density alternative at roughly 50% of the Preferred Plan's building area, emphasizing light industrial uses (about 50% more than the Preferred Plan) while significantly reducing office and retail space and eliminating hotel, residential, and separate parking structure uses.

value.name: City Manager's Plan (CCM Plan)
value.description: A plan similar in overall scale to the Preferred Plan but deleting residential uses and significantly increasing the emphasis on technical office and light industrial uses; provides more jobs in all categories than the Preferred Plan and includes a 266-room hotel.

value.name: Maximum Plan
value.description: The highest-density alternative, approximately 30% greater than existing adjacent development such as Technology Square, featuring a 400-room hotel and 606 residential units; deletes the Central Corridor between Main Street and Broadway to accommodate increased development density.

value.name: No Build Plan
value.description: The no-action alternative requiring only completion of demolition and relocation programs and minimum utility and street improvements, with both the quadrangle and triangle left as landscaped open space and no new development on the project site.

subthemes: Urban Expansion and Land Use Change

themes.justification: The project_description and alternatives_overview describe a large-scale urban renewal effort involving mixed-use redevelopment, housing, and major street improvements, while the public_response and affected_community fields document significant community opposition, displacement concerns, and conflicts over working-class character and low-income residents.
